name: Ad-hoc Polymorphism

laws: One name, several type-specific implementations selected by argument type(s)
Resolution may be static (overload, traits) or dynamic
Distinct from parametric polymorphism (one impl for all types)
x.encoding: Function/operator overloading (C++, C#, Java), trait/typeclass instances (Rust traits, Haskell classes). C lacks overloading (use _Generic or distinct names).

def: Polymorphism where a single name has multiple type-specific implementations chosen by the operand types — overloading and typeclass/trait dispatch.
problem: Use one intuitive name (+, print, to_string) across unrelated types without a single uniform implementation.
mechanism: The compiler (or instance resolution) selects the implementation matching the argument types. Overloading resolves syntactically; typeclasses/traits resolve by satisfying an interface for the type. Contrast parametric polymorphism, which has one body for all types.
benefits: Static dispatch → zero-cost in many languages
limits: Implicit selection hurts readability when abused
failures: Overload sets that pick an unexpected candidate
whenNot: Behaviour should be uniform across types (use generics)
myths: "Polymorphism = inheritance" — ad-hoc (overloading/traits) is a distinct, often static form
